While instructional video corpora, like HowTo100M, offer rich resources for model training, they present significant challenges, including noisy ASR transcripts and inconsistent temporal alignments between narration and visual content. In this work, we introduce an automated, training-free pipeline to extract high-quality procedural annotations from in-the-wild instructional videos. Our approach segments videos into coherent shots, filters poorly aligned content, and leverages state-of-the-art multimodal and large language models (Qwen2.5-VL and DeepSeek-R1) to generate structured, temporally grounded procedural steps.
  This pipeline yields DenseStep2M, a large-scale dataset comprising approximately 100K videos and 2M detailed instructional steps, designed to support comprehensive long-form video understanding. To rigorously evaluate our pipeline, we curate DenseCaption100, a benchmark of high-quality, human-written captions. Evaluations demonstrate strong alignment between our auto-generated steps and human annotations. Furthermore, we validate the utility of DenseStep2M across three core downstream tasks: dense video captioning, procedural step grounding, and cross-modal retrieval. Models fine-tuned on DenseStep2M achieve substantial gains in captioning quality and temporal localization, while exhibiting robust zero-shot generalization across egocentric, exocentric, and mixed-perspective domains. These results underscore the effectiveness of DenseStep2M in facilitating advanced multimodal alignment and long-term activity reasoning. Our dataset is available at https://huggingface.co/datasets/mingjige/DenseStep2M.

AI.tldr: Introduces DenseStep2M, a large-scale dataset of ~100K videos with 2M procedural steps generated via a training-free pipeline using Qwen2.5-VL and DeepSeek-R1, plus DenseCaption100 benchmark. Demonstrates strong gains in dense video captioning, procedural step grounding, and cross-modal retrieval.
AI.motivation: Long-term video understanding is challenged by noisy ASR transcripts and inconsistent temporal alignments in instructional video corpora like HowTo100M. The authors aim to extract clean, high-quality procedural annotations without human supervision.
AI.method: An automated, training-free pipeline: (1) segment videos into coherent shots, (2) filter poorly aligned content, (3) leverage Qwen2.5-VL (multimodal) and DeepSeek-R1 (LLM) to generate structured, temporally grounded procedural steps.
AI.result: Produces DenseStep2M (~100K videos, 2M steps). Models fine-tuned on it achieve substantial gains in captioning quality and temporal localization, with robust zero-shot generalization across egocentric, exocentric, and mixed-perspective domains.
AI.conclusion: DenseStep2M effectively facilitates multimodal alignment and long-term activity reasoning, serving as a high-quality resource for long-form video understanding tasks.

title: AirZoo: A Unified Large-Scale Dataset for Grounding Aerial Geometric 3D Vision
summary: Despite the rapid progress in data-driven 3D vision, aerial geometric 3D vision remains a formidable challenge due to the severe scarcity of large-scale, high-fidelity training data. Existing benchmarks, predominantly biased toward ground-level or object-centric views, do not account for complex viewpoint transformations and diverse environmental conditions in UAV-based sensing. To bridge this critical gap, we propose AirZoo, a unified large-scale dataset and benchmark for grounding aerial geometric 3D vision. AirZoo possesses three appealing properties: 1) Scalable Generation Pipeline: Leveraging freely available, world-scale photogrammetric 3D meshes, it renders vast outdoor environments with customizable UAV flight trajectories and configurable weather/illumination. 2) Comprehensive Scene Diversity: It provides the most extensive coverage of region types to date (spanning 378 regions across 22 countries), systematically encompassing both highly structured urban landscapes and complex unstructured natural environments. 3) Rich Geometric Annotations: Each frame provides synchronized, pixel-level metric depth and precise 6-DoF geo-referenced poses, essential for geometry-aware learning. Through three rigorous evaluation tracks -- aerial image retrieval, cross-view matching, and multi-view 3D reconstruction -- we demonstrate that AirZoo serves as a powerful pre-training engine. Extensive experiments on both public and newly collected real-world benchmarks reveal that fine-tuning on AirZoo yields substantial performance gains for SoTA models (e.g., MegaLoc, RoMa, VGGT, and Depth Anything 3), establishing a new performance upper bound for aerial spatial intelligence.

AI.tldr: AirZoo is a large-scale dataset and benchmark for aerial geometric 3D vision, generated from photogrammetric 3D meshes with customizable UAV trajectories and weather/illumination, covering 378 regions across 22 countries. It provides pixel-level depth and 6-DoF poses, and fine-tuning on AirZoo boosts SoTA models (MegaLoc, RoMa, VGGT, Depth Anything 3) on aerial tasks.
AI.motivation: Existing 3D vision datasets are biased toward ground-level or object-centric views and lack the complex viewpoint transformations and environmental diversity needed for UAV-based aerial geometric 3D vision. AirZoo bridges this gap by providing a scalable, diverse, and richly annotated aerial dataset.
AI.method: AirZoo uses freely available world-scale photogrammetric 3D meshes to render outdoor environments with customizable UAV flight trajectories and configurable weather/illumination. It provides synchronized pixel-level metric depth and 6-DoF geo-referenced poses, evaluated across three tracks: aerial image retrieval, cross-view matching, and multi-view 3D reconstruction.
AI.result: Fine-tuning on AirZoo yields substantial performance gains for SoTA models (MegaLoc, RoMa, VGGT, Depth Anything 3), establishing a new performance upper bound for aerial spatial intelligence on both public and newly collected real-world benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: AirZoo serves as a powerful pre-training engine for aerial geometric 3D vision, addressing data scarcity and enabling state-of-the-art performance across multiple aerial tasks.

title: Star-Fusion: A Multi-modal Transformer Architecture for Discrete Celestial Orientation via Spherical Topology
summary: Reliable celestial attitude determination is a critical requirement for autonomous spacecraft navigation, yet traditional "Lost-in-Space" (LIS) algorithms often suffer from high computational overhead and sensitivity to sensor-induced noise. While deep learning has emerged as a promising alternative, standard regression models are often confounded by the non-Euclidean topology of the celestial sphere and by the periodic boundary conditions of Right Ascension (RA) and Declination (Dec). In this paper, we present Star-Fusion, a multi-modal architecture that reformulates orientation estimation as a discrete topological classification task. Our approach leverages spherical K-Means clustering to partition the celestial sphere into K topologically consistent regions, effectively mitigating coordinate wrapping artifacts. The proposed architecture employs a tripartite fusion strategy: a SwinV2-Tiny transformer backbone for photometric feature extraction, a convolutional heatmap branch for spatial grounding, and a coordinate-based MLP for geometric anchoring. Experimental evaluations on a synthetic Hipparcos-derived dataset demonstrate that Star-Fusion achieves a Top-1 accuracy of 93.4% and a Top-3 accuracy of 97.8%. Furthermore, the model exhibits high computational efficiency, maintaining an inference latency of 18.4 ms on resource-constrained COTS hardware, making it a viable candidate for real-time onboard deployment in next-generation satellite constellations.

AI.tldr: Star-Fusion is a multi-modal deep learning architecture that reformulates spacecraft celestial attitude determination as a discrete topological classification task using spherical K-Means clustering and a tripartite fusion strategy (SwinV2-Tiny transformer, convolutional heatmap branch, coordinate-based MLP), achieving 93.4% Top-1 accuracy and 18.4 ms inference latency on COTS hardware.
AI.motivation: Traditional "Lost-in-Space" (LIS) algorithms suffer from high computational overhead and sensitivity to sensor noise, while deep learning regression models are confounded by the non-Euclidean topology of the celestial sphere and periodic boundary conditions of Right Ascension/Declination.
AI.method: Star-Fusion uses spherical K-Means clustering to partition the celestial sphere into K topologically consistent regions, reformulating orientation estimation as discrete classification. It employs a tripartite architecture: (1) SwinV2-Tiny transformer for photometric features, (2) convolutional heatmap branch for spatial grounding, and (3) coordinate-based MLP for geometric anchoring.
AI.result: On a synthetic Hipparcos-derived dataset, Star-Fusion achieves 93.4% Top-1 accuracy and 97.8% Top-3 accuracy, with an inference latency of 18.4 ms on resource-constrained COTS hardware.
AI.conclusion: Star-Fusion is a viable candidate for real-time onboard deployment in next-generation satellite constellations, offering high accuracy and computational efficiency for autonomous spacecraft navigation.

title: FunFace: Feature Utility and Norm Estimation for Face Recognition
summary: Face Recognition (FR) is used in a variety of application domains, from entertainment and banking to security and surveillance. Such applications rely on the FR model to be robust and perform well in a variety of settings. To achieve this, state-of-the-art FR models typically use expressive adaptive margin loss functions, which tie the feature norm to concepts related to sample quality, such as recognizability and perceptual image quality. Recently, through the development of Face Image Quality Assessment (FIQA) techniques, biometric utility has become the preferred measure of face-image quality and has been shown to be a better predictor of the usefulness of samples for face recognition compared to more human-centric aspects, such as resolution, blur, and lighting, tied to general image quality. While image quality expressed through feature norms exhibits a certain level of correlation with biometric utility, it does not fully encapsulate all aspects of utility. To address this point, we propose a new adaptive margin loss, FunFace (Face Recognition Through Utility and Norm Estimation), which incorporates biometric utility, estimated by the Certainty Ratio, into the adaptive margin, taking inspiration from AdaFace. We show that FunFace (when used to train a face recognition model) achieves competitive results to other state-of-the-art FR models on benchmarks containing high-quality samples, while surpassing them on low quality benchmarks.

AI.tldr: FunFace proposes a new adaptive margin loss function for face recognition that incorporates biometric utility (estimated via Certainty Ratio) into feature norm-based margins, achieving competitive results on high-quality benchmarks and surpassing SOTA on low-quality benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Existing adaptive margin loss functions tie feature norms to sample quality but fail to fully capture biometric utility—the actual usefulness of a sample for face recognition. The authors aim to bridge this gap by integrating utility-based quality measures into the loss function.
AI.method: FunFace introduces an adaptive margin loss that incorporates biometric utility estimated by the Certainty Ratio (a FIQA metric) into the margin formulation, inspired by AdaFace. This links the margin not only to feature norms but also to actual sample utility.
AI.result: FunFace achieves competitive results with state-of-the-art FR models on high-quality benchmarks and outperforms them on low-quality benchmarks, demonstrating better robustness in challenging conditions.
AI.conclusion: Incorporating biometric utility into adaptive margin losses improves face recognition performance, especially on low-quality samples, suggesting that utility-based quality is a more effective measure for guiding FR training than feature norms alone.

title: State Beyond Appearance: Diagnosing and Improving State Consistency in Dial-Based Measurement Reading
summary: Multimodal large language models (MLLMs) have achieved impressive progress on general multimodal tasks, yet they remain brittle on dial-based measurement reading. In this paper, we study this problem through controlled benchmarks and feature-space probing, and show that current MLLMs not only achieve unsatisfactory accuracy on dial-based readout, but also suffer sharp performance drops under viewpoint and illumination changes even when the underlying dial state remains fixed. Our probing analysis further reveals that same-state samples under appearance variation are not consistently clustered, while neighboring states fail to preserve the local structure implied by continuous dial values. These findings suggest that existing MLLMs largely ignore the intrinsic state geometry of dial measurement tasks and instead rely on superficial appearance cues. Motivated by this diagnosis, we propose TriSCA, a tri-level state-consistent alignment framework for dial-based measurement reading. Specifically, TriSCA consists of state-distance-aware representation alignment, metadata-grounded observation-to-state supervision, and state-aware objective alignment. Extensive ablation studies and evaluation experiments on controlled clock and gauge benchmarks, together with evaluation on an external real-world benchmark, demonstrate the effectiveness of our method.

AI.tldr: MLLMs struggle with dial-based measurement reading due to relying on superficial appearance cues rather than intrinsic state geometry. The proposed TriSCA framework uses tri-level state-consistent alignment to significantly improve performance under viewpoint/illumination changes.
AI.motivation: Current MLLMs achieve unsatisfactory accuracy on dial-based readout and suffer sharp performance drops under viewpoint/illumination changes, because they ignore the intrinsic state geometry of dial measurement tasks and rely on superficial appearance cues.
AI.method: TriSCA: a tri-level state-consistent alignment framework comprising (1) state-distance-aware representation alignment, (2) metadata-grounded observation-to-state supervision, and (3) state-aware objective alignment.
AI.result: Extensive ablation studies and evaluations on controlled clock/gauge benchmarks and an external real-world benchmark demonstrate the effectiveness of TriSCA.
AI.conclusion: Existing MLLMs fail to preserve local structure and state geometry in dial reading tasks; TriSCA effectively addresses these limitations through state-consistent alignment.

title: SnapPose3D: Diffusion-Based Single-Frame 2D-to-3D Lifting of Human Poses
summary: Depth ambiguity and joint uncertainty are the two main obstacles in obtaining accurate human pose predictions by 2D-to-3D lifting methods proposed in the literature. In particular, these issues are caused by 2D joint locations that can be mapped to multiple 3D positions, inducing multiple possible final poses. Following these considerations, we propose leveraging diffusion-based models generation capability to predict multiple hypotheses and aggregate them in a final accurate pose. Therefore, we introduce SnapPose3D, a pose-lifting framework trained deterministically to denoise 3D poses conditioned on both visual context and 2D pose features. SnapPose3D adopts a probabilistic approach during inference, generating multiple hypotheses through random sampling from a unit Gaussian distribution. Unlike most previous methods that address pose ambiguity by processing temporal sequences, SnapPose3D uses single frames as input, avoiding tracking and limiting computational cost, data acquisition complexity, and the need for online, real-time applications. We extensively evaluate SnapPose3D on well-known benchmarks for the 3D human pose estimation task showing its ability to generate and aggregate accurate hypotheses that lead to state-of-the-art results.

AI.tldr: SnapPose3D is a diffusion-based single-frame 3D human pose lifting framework that generates multiple pose hypotheses from 2D keypoints and aggregates them for state-of-the-art accuracy, addressing depth ambiguity and joint uncertainty without requiring temporal sequences.
AI.motivation: Depth ambiguity and joint uncertainty in 2D-to-3D pose lifting cause multiple possible 3D poses from the same 2D input. The authors aim to address these issues without relying on temporal sequences (which increase computational cost and require tracking).
AI.method: SnapPose3D uses a diffusion-based pose-lifting framework trained deterministically to denoise 3D poses conditioned on both visual context and 2D pose features. During inference, it adopts a probabilistic approach by generating multiple hypotheses through random sampling from a unit Gaussian distribution, then aggregates them into a final accurate pose.
AI.result: Extensive evaluation on well-known 3D human pose estimation benchmarks shows SnapPose3D generates and aggregates accurate hypotheses, achieving state-of-the-art results.
AI.conclusion: The diffusion-based, single-frame approach effectively handles pose ambiguity and achieves competitive accuracy while avoiding the computational overhead and tracking requirements of temporal methods.

title: SynSur: An end-to-end generative pipeline for synthetic industrial surface defect generation and detection
summary: The bottleneck in learning-based industrial defect detection is often limited not by model capacity, but by the scarcity of labeled defect data: defects are rare, annotations are expensive, and collecting balanced training sets is slow. We present an end-to-end pipeline for synthetic defect generation and annotation, combining Vision-Language-Model-based prompts, LoRA-adapted diffusion, mask-guided inpainting, and sample filtering with automatic label derivation, and demonstrates the potential of real data with realistic synthetic samples to overcome data scarcity. The evaluation is conducted on, a challenging dataset of pitting defects on ball screw drives, and then on a subset of the Mobile phone screen surface defect segmentation dataset (MSD) dataset to test cross-domain transfer. Beyond downstream detector performance, we analyze key stages of the pipeline, including prompt construction, LoRA selection, and sample filtering with DreamSim and CLIPScore, to understand which synthetic samples are both realistic and useful. Experiments with YOLOv26, YOLOX, and LW-DETR show that synthetic-only training does not replace real data. When combined with real data, synthetic defects can preserve performance and yield modest gains in selected BSData training regimes. The MSD transfer study shows that the overall pipeline structure carries over to a second industrial inspection domain, while also highlighting the importance of domain-specific adaptation and annotation-quality control. Overall, the paper provides an end-to-end assessment of diffusion-based industrial defect synthesis and shows that its strongest value lies in strengthening scarce real datasets rather than substituting for them.

AI.tldr: The paper presents an end-to-end pipeline using Vision-Language-Model prompts, LoRA-adapted diffusion, and mask-guided inpainting to generate synthetic industrial defect data. Experiments show synthetic data cannot replace real data but can modestly improve defect detection performance when combined with limited real samples.
AI.motivation: Industrial defect detection is bottlenecked by scarcity of labeled defect data due to rare occurrences, expensive annotations, and difficulty collecting balanced training sets.
AI.method: An end-to-end pipeline combining VLM-based prompts, LoRA-adapted diffusion models, mask-guided inpainting for synthetic defect generation, plus DreamSim/CLIPScore-based filtering and automatic label derivation.
AI.result: Synthetic-only training does not replace real data. When combined with real data, synthetic defects preserve performance and yield modest gains in selected training regimes. Cross-domain transfer to MSD dataset shows pipeline structure carries over but requires domain-specific adaptation.
AI.conclusion: The strongest value of diffusion-based industrial defect synthesis lies in strengthening scarce real datasets rather than substituting for them.

title: Hearing the Room Through the Shape of the Drum: Modal-Guided Sound Recovery from Multi-Point Surface Vibrations
comment: Oral presentation at The IEEE/CVF Conference on Computer Vision and Pattern Recognition 2026
summary: Optical vibration sensing enables recovering the scene sound directly from the surface vibration of nearby objects, turning everyday objects into ``visual microphones''. However, most prior methods had focused on capturing the vibrations of specific objects with highly favorable vibration responses. These include objects where the surface vibrations are generated by the object itself (e.g., speaker membrane or guitar body) or objects consisting of a thin membrane which is highly reactive to sound (e.g., a chip bag or the leaf of a plant). In this paper, we tackle sound recovery for a more challenging class of solid objects whose vibration responses are poor or highly resonant. We simultaneously capture vibrations for multiple surface points on the object using a speckle-based vibrometry imaging system. Then, we derive a novel physics-guided vibration formation model that relates the scene sound source to the captured multi-point multi-axis vibrations via the object's vibrational modes. The model is then used to reverse the resonant transfer function of the vibrating object, fusing multiple vibration signals to estimate the original sound source in the scene. We evaluate our approach by recovering sound from a variety of everyday objects, demonstrating that it significantly outperforms traditional single-point speckle vibrometry in challenging scenarios and other signal-processing-based methods for multi-signal fusing.

AI.tldr: This paper recovers sound from solid objects with poor vibration responses by capturing multi-point multi-axis vibrations using speckle vibrometry and reversing the resonant transfer function via a physics-guided vibration formation model.
AI.motivation: Prior visual microphone methods work well only on highly responsive objects (e.g., membranes, chip bags). This paper tackles sound recovery from challenging solid objects that have poor or highly resonant vibration responses.
AI.method: Uses a speckle-based vibrometry system to capture multi-point multi-axis vibrations, derives a physics-guided vibration formation model linking sound source to vibrations via vibrational modes, then reverses the resonant transfer function by fusing multiple signals to estimate the original sound.
AI.result: The proposed approach significantly outperforms traditional single-point speckle vibrometry and other signal-processing-based multi-signal fusion methods on various everyday solid objects.
AI.conclusion: Multi-point multi-axis vibration sensing combined with a physics-guided model enables sound recovery from challenging solid objects that were previously unsuitable for visual microphone techniques.

title: CurEvo: Curriculum-Guided Self-Evolution for Video Understanding
summary: Recent advances in self-evolution video understanding frameworks have demonstrated the potential of autonomous learning without human annotations. However, existing methods often suffer from weakly controlled optimization and uncontrolled difficulty progression, as they lack structured guidance throughout the iterative learning process. To address these limitations, we propose CurEvo, a curriculum-guided self-evolution framework that introduces curriculum learning into self-evolution to achieve more structured and progressive model improvement. CurEvo dynamically regulates task difficulty, refines evaluation criteria, and balances data diversity according to model competence, forming a curriculum-guided feedback loop that aligns learning complexity with model capability. Built upon this principle, we develop a multi-dimensional adaptive QA framework that jointly evolves question generation and answer evaluation across perception, recognition, and understanding dimensions, ensuring coherent and measurable curriculum progression. Through this integration, CurEvo transforms weakly controlled self-evolution into a more structured learning process for autonomous video understanding. Across seven backbones, CurEvo consistently improves both benchmark accuracy and evaluator-based semantic score on four VideoQA benchmarks, validating the effectiveness of curriculum-guided self-evolution for video understanding.

AI.tldr: CurEvo introduces curriculum learning into self-evolution frameworks for video understanding, dynamically adjusting task difficulty and evaluation criteria to create structured, progressive model improvement without human annotations.
AI.motivation: Existing self-evolution video understanding frameworks suffer from weakly controlled optimization and uncontrolled difficulty progression due to lack of structured guidance throughout iterative learning.
AI.method: Proposes CurEvo, a curriculum-guided self-evolution framework that dynamically regulates task difficulty, refines evaluation criteria, and balances data diversity according to model competence, with a multi-dimensional adaptive QA framework evolving question generation and answer evaluation across perception, recognition, and understanding dimensions.
AI.result: Across seven backbones, CurEvo consistently improves both benchmark accuracy and evaluator-based semantic score on four VideoQA benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: Curriculum-guided self-evolution effectively transforms weakly controlled self-evolution into a more structured learning process, validating its effectiveness for autonomous video understanding.

title: Learning Sparse BRDF Measurement Samples from Image
summary: Accurate BRDF acquisition is important for realistic rendering, but dense gonioreflectometer measurements are slow and expensive. We study how to select a small number of BRDF measurements that are most useful for reconstructing material appearance under a learned reflectance prior. Our method combines a set encoder for sparse coordinate-value observations, a pretrained hypernetwork-based BRDF reconstructor, and a differentiable renderer. During sampler training, the reconstructor is kept fixed and gradients from BRDF-space and rendered-image losses are used to optimize measurement locations. This separates sample selection from prior fitting and encourages the sampler to choose directions that are informative under the learned material distribution. Experiments on the MERL dataset show that the proposed sampler improves low-budget reconstruction quality at 8 and 16 measurements compared with neural reconstruction baselines, while PCA-based methods remain strong at larger budgets. We further analyze the effect of image-space supervision, co-optimization, and image-only latent fitting for unseen materials.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a learned sampling strategy for BRDF acquisition that selects optimal measurement directions using a hypernetwork-based reconstructor and differentiable rendering, achieving better reconstruction at low budgets (8-16 measurements) compared to neural baselines.
AI.motivation: BRDF acquisition is slow and expensive with dense gonioreflectometer measurements; the authors aim to select a small number of informative measurements to reconstruct material appearance efficiently.
AI.method: The method combines a set encoder for sparse coordinate-value observations, a pretrained hypernetwork-based BRDF reconstructor, and a differentiable renderer. During sampler training, the reconstructor is fixed, and gradients from BRDF-space and rendered-image losses optimize measurement locations.
AI.result: On the MERL dataset, the proposed sampler improves reconstruction quality at 8 and 16 measurements over neural reconstruction baselines, though PCA-based methods remain strong at larger budgets.
AI.conclusion: Separating sample selection from prior fitting helps select informative directions under learned material distribution; the approach is effective for low-budget BRDF acquisition.

title: GLM-5V-Turbo: Toward a Native Foundation Model for Multimodal Agents
summary: We present GLM-5V-Turbo, a step toward native foundation models for multimodal agents. As foundation models are increasingly deployed in real environments, agentic capability depends not only on language reasoning, but also on the ability to perceive, interpret, and act over heterogeneous contexts such as images, videos, webpages, documents, GUIs. GLM-5V-Turbo is built around this objective: multimodal perception is integrated as a core component of reasoning, planning, tool use, and execution, rather than as an auxiliary interface to a language model. This report summarizes the main improvements behind GLM-5V-Turbo across model design, multimodal training, reinforcement learning, toolchain expansion, and integration with agent frameworks. These developments lead to strong performance in multimodal coding, visual tool use, and framework-based agentic tasks, while preserving competitive text-only coding capability. More importantly, our development process offers practical insights for building multimodal agents, highlighting the central role of multimodal perception, hierarchical optimization, and reliable end-to-end verification.

AI.tldr: GLM-5V-Turbo is a multimodal foundation model designed for agentic tasks, integrating perception, reasoning, planning, tool use, and execution natively rather than as an auxiliary module, achieving strong performance across multimodal coding, visual tool use, and agentic tasks.
AI.motivation: To address the limitation that current foundation models treat multimodal perception as an auxiliary interface rather than a core component, hindering their agentic capabilities in real-world heterogeneous environments involving images, videos, webpages, documents, and GUIs.
AI.method: Integrates multimodal perception as a core component of reasoning, planning, tool use, and execution, with improvements in model design, multimodal training, reinforcement learning, toolchain expansion, and agent framework integration.
AI.result: Strong performance in multimodal coding, visual tool use, and framework-based agentic tasks, while maintaining competitive text-only coding capabilities.
AI.conclusion: Multimodal perception, hierarchical optimization, and reliable end-to-end verification are central to building effective multimodal agents.

title: TAP into the Patch Tokens: Leveraging Vision Foundation Model Features for AI-Generated Image Detection
comment: This paper has been accepted at IEEE/CVF Conference on Computer Vision and Pattern Recognition Workshops (CVPRW), 2026
summary: Recent methods demonstrate that large-scale pretrained models, such as CLIP vision transformers, effectively detect AI-generated images (AIGIs) from unseen generative models when used as feature extractors. Many state-of-the-art methods for AI-generated image detection build upon the original CLIP-ViT to enhance this generalization. Since CLIP's release, numerous vision foundation models (VFMs) have emerged, incorporating architectural improvements and different training paradigms. Despite these advances, their potential for AIGI detection and AI image forensics remains largely unexplored. In this work, we present a comprehensive benchmark across multiple VFM families, covering diverse pretraining objectives, input resolutions, and model scales. We systematically evaluate their out-of-the-box performance for detecting fully-generated AI-images and AI-inpainted images, and discover that the best model outperforms the original CLIP by more than 12% in accuracy, beating established approaches in the process. To fully leverage the features of a modern VFM, we propose a simple redesign of the classifier head by utilizing tunable attention pooling (TAP), which aggregates output tokens into a refined global representation. Integrating TAP with the latest VFMs yields substantial performance gains across several AIGI detection benchmarks, establishing a new state-of-the-art on two challenging benchmarks for in-the-wild detection of AI-generated and -inpainted images.

AI.tldr: This paper benchmarks various vision foundation models (VFMs) for AI-generated image detection, finding modern VFMs outperform original CLIP by 12%+ in accuracy, and proposes a tunable attention pooling (TAP) head that achieves state-of-the-art results on challenging benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Despite numerous advanced vision foundation models (VFMs) being developed after CLIP, their potential for AI-generated image detection and forensics has remained largely unexplored.
AI.method: 1) Comprehensive benchmarking of multiple VFM families (diverse pretraining objectives, resolutions, scales) for AIGI detection; 2) A simple redesign of the classifier head using tunable attention pooling (TAP) to aggregate output tokens into refined global representation.
AI.result: The best VFM outperforms original CLIP by >12% accuracy, beating established approaches. TAP integrated with modern VFMs yields substantial gains and establishes new state-of-the-art on two challenging in-the-wild benchmarks for AI-generated and AI-inpainted image detection.
AI.conclusion: Modern VFMs significantly improve AI-generated image detection over CLIP, and the proposed TAP head further boosts performance, setting new SOTA results.

title: MemOVCD: Training-Free Open-Vocabulary Change Detection via Cross-Temporal Memory Reasoning and Global-Local Adaptive Rectification
summary: Open-vocabulary change detection aims to identify semantic changes in bi-temporal remote sensing images without predefined categories. Recent methods combine foundation models such as SAM, DINO and CLIP, but typically process each timestamp independently or interact only at the final comparison stage. Such paradigms suffer from insufficient temporal coupling during semantic reasoning, which limits their ability to distinguish genuine semantic changes from non-semantic appearance discrepancies. In addition, patch-dominant inference on high-resolution images often weakens global semantic continuity and produces fragmented change regions. To address these issues, we propose MemOVCD, a training-free open-vocabulary change detection framework based on cross-temporal memory reasoning and global-local adaptive rectification. Specifically, we reformulate bi-temporal change detection as a two-frame tracking problem and introduce weighted bidirectional propagation to aggregate semantic evidence from both temporal directions. To stabilize memory propagation across large temporal gaps, we construct histogram-aligned transition frames to smooth abrupt appearance changes. Moreover, a global-local adaptive rectification strategy adaptively fuses local and global-view predictions, improving spatial consistency while preserving fine-grained details. Experiments on five benchmarks demonstrate that MemOVCD achieves favorable performance on two change detection tasks, validating its effectiveness and generalization under diverse open-vocabulary settings.

AI.tldr: MemOVCD is a training-free open-vocabulary change detection framework that reformulates bi-temporal change detection as a two-frame tracking problem with weighted bidirectional propagation, histogram-aligned transition frames, and global-local adaptive rectification to improve semantic change detection accuracy and spatial consistency.
AI.motivation: Existing open-vocabulary change detection methods process each timestamp independently or interact only at the final stage, leading to insufficient temporal coupling during semantic reasoning and inability to distinguish genuine semantic changes from non-semantic appearance discrepancies. Patch-dominant inference also weakens global semantic continuity and produces fragmented change regions.
AI.method: (1) Reformulates bi-temporal change detection as a two-frame tracking problem with weighted bidirectional propagation to aggregate semantic evidence from both temporal directions. (2) Constructs histogram-aligned transition frames to stabilize memory propagation across large temporal gaps. (3) Global-local adaptive rectification strategy adaptively fuses local and global-view predictions for better spatial consistency.
AI.result: Experiments on five benchmarks demonstrate favorable performance on two change detection tasks, validating effectiveness and generalization under diverse open-vocabulary settings.
AI.conclusion: MemOVCD effectively addresses temporal coupling and spatial consistency issues in open-vocabulary change detection through cross-temporal memory reasoning and global-local adaptive rectification, achieving strong performance without requiring training.

title: Virtual-reality based patient-specific simulation of spine surgical procedures: A fast, highly automated and high-fidelity system for surgical education and planning
summary: Surgical training involves didactic teaching, mentor-led learning, surgical skills laboratories, and direct exposure to surgery; however, increasing clinical pressures have limited operating room (OR) exposure. This work leverages virtual reality (VR) to provide a safe and immersive training environment. Existing VR training is often based on standardized scenarios not tailored to individual clinical cases. This study addresses this limitation using artificial intelligence (AI) based computer vision methods to generate patient-specific simulations from computed tomography (CT) and magnetic resonance imaging (MRI). This study focuses on patient-specific spinal decompression simulation for spinal stenosis in a virtual operating room. The objectives were (1) automatic creation of 3D anatomical models and (2) VR simulation of spinal decompression procedures including laminectomy, disc resection, and foraminotomy. Model construction required multimodal fusion (registration) of CT and MRI and segmentation of relevant structures. Segmentation was evaluated using the Dice Similarity Coefficient (DSC), and registration accuracy using Target Registration Error (TRE). Qualitative feedback was obtained from surgeons and trainees. High-fidelity patient-specific 3D models were generated efficiently (approximately 2.5 minutes per case, N = 15). Segmentation accuracy was high, with a DSC of 0.95 (+/- 0.03) for vertebral bone and 0.895 (+/- 0.02) for soft tissue structures. Registration accuracy showed a mean TRE of 1.73 (+/- 0.42) mm. Semi-structured interviews indicated improved spatial understanding, increased procedural confidence, and strong perceived educational value. This platform significantly reduced the time and costs of patient-specific modelling, thereby facilitating pre-operative planning, post-procedural assessments, and comprehensive surgical simulation.

AI.tldr: This paper presents an AI-powered VR system that automatically creates patient-specific 3D anatomical models from CT/MRI scans for spinal decompression surgery training, achieving high segmentation accuracy (DSC 0.95 for bone) and fast processing (~2.5 min/case).
AI.motivation: Increasing clinical pressures limit OR exposure for surgical trainees; existing VR training uses standardized scenarios that don't cover individual clinical cases.
AI.method: AI-based computer vision methods for multimodal fusion (CT+MRI registration), automatic segmentation of anatomical structures, and VR simulation of spinal decompression procedures (laminectomy, disc resection, foraminotomy).
AI.result: High-fidelity 3D models generated in ~2.5 min per case (N=15); DSC 0.95±0.03 for bone, 0.895±0.02 for soft tissue; TRE 1.73±0.42 mm; positive qualitative feedback from surgeons.
AI.conclusion: The platform reduces time and costs of patient-specific modeling, facilitating pre-operative planning, post-procedural assessments, and comprehensive surgical simulation.

title: MesonGS++: Post-training Compression of 3D Gaussian Splatting with Hyperparameter Searching
summary: 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS) achieves high-quality novel view synthesis with real-time rendering, but its storage cost remains prohibitive for practical deployment. Existing post-training compression methods still rely on many coupled hyperparameters across pruning, transformation, quantization, and entropy coding, making it difficult to control the final compressed size and fully exploit the rate-distortion trade-off. We propose MesonGS++, a size-aware post-training codec for 3D Gaussian compression. On the codec side, MesonGS++ combines joint importance-based pruning, octree geometry coding, attribute transformation, selective vector quantization for higher-degree spherical harmonics, and group-wise mixed-precision quantization with entropy coding. On the configuration side, it treats the reserve ratio and bit-width allocation as the dominant rate-distortion knobs and jointly optimizes them under a target storage budget via discrete sampling and 0--1 integer linear programming. We further propose a linear size estimator and a CUDA parallel quantization operator to accelerate the hyperparameter searching process. Extensive experiments show that MesonGS++ achieves over 34$\times$ compression while preserving rendering fidelity, outperforming state-of-the-art post-training methods and accurately meeting target size budgets. Remarkably, without any training, MesonGS++ can even surpass the PSNR of vanilla 3DGS at a 20$\times$ compression rate on the Stump scene. Our code is available at https://github.com/mmlab-sigs/mesongs_plus

AI.tldr: MesonGS++ is a size-aware post-training compression codec for 3D Gaussian Splatting that achieves over 34x compression while preserving rendering quality, using joint importance pruning, octree coding, vector quantization, and mixed-precision quantization, with rate-distortion knobs optimized via discrete sampling and integer linear programming.
AI.motivation: 3DGS achieves high-quality real-time novel view synthesis but suffers from prohibitive storage costs. Existing post-training compression methods rely on many coupled hyperparameters across multiple stages, making it difficult to control the final compressed size or fully exploit the rate-distortion trade-off.
AI.method: MesonGS++ combines: (1) joint importance-based pruning, (2) octree geometry coding, (3) attribute transformation, (4) selective vector quantization for higher-degree spherical harmonics, (5) group-wise mixed-precision quantization with entropy coding. It treats reserve ratio and bit-width allocation as dominant rate-distortion knobs, jointly optimized via discrete sampling and 0-1 integer linear programming under a target storage budget. A linear size estimator and CUDA parallel quantization operator accelerate hyperparameter search.
AI.result: Over 34x compression while preserving rendering fidelity, outperforming state-of-the-art post-training methods and accurately meeting target size budgets. On the Stump scene, it surpasses vanilla 3DGS PSNR at 20x compression without any training.
AI.conclusion: MesonGS++ demonstrates effective size-aware post-training compression of 3D Gaussians with controllable rate-distortion trade-off, achieving superior compression ratios while maintaining or even improving rendering quality.

title: ViCrop-Det: Spatial Attention Entropy Guided Cropping for Training-Free Small-Object Detection
summary: Transformer-based architectures have established a dominant paradigm in global semantic perception; however, they remain fundamentally constrained by the profound spatial heterogeneity inherent in natural images. Specifically, the imposition of a uniform global receptive field across regions of varying information density inevitably leads to local feature degradation, particularly in dense conflict zones populated by microscopic targets. To address this mechanistic limitation, we propose ViCrop-Det, a training-free inference framework that introduces adaptive spatial trust region shrinkage. Inspired by the use of attention entropy in anomaly segmentation, ViCrop-Det leverages the detection decoder's cross-attention distribution as an endogenous probe. By utilizing Spatial Attention Entropy (SAE) to heuristically evaluate local spatial ambiguity, the framework executes dynamic spatial routing, allocating a fixed computational budget exclusively to regions exhibiting both high target saliency and high cognitive uncertainty. By shrinking the spatial trust region and injecting high-frequency localized observations, ViCrop-Det actively resolves spatial ambiguity and recovers fine-grained features without requiring architectural modifications. Extensive evaluations on VisDrone and DOTA-v1.5 demonstrate that ViCrop-Det yields competitive performance enhancements, consistently adding +1-3 mAP@50 to RT-DETR-R50 and Deformable DETR with a marginal 20-23\% latency overhead. On MS COCO, $AP_{S}$ improves while $AP_{M}/AP_{L}$ remains stable, indicating precise fine-scale refinement without compromising the global spatial prior. Under compute-matched settings, our adaptive routing strategy comprehensively surpasses uniform slicing baselines, achieving a highly optimized accuracy-speed trade-off.

AI.tldr: ViCrop-Det is a training-free inference framework that adaptively shrinks spatial attention regions using attention entropy to resolve local feature degradation in dense detection scenarios, achieving +1-3 mAP@50 on aerial benchmarks with minimal latency overhead.
AI.motivation: Transformer-based detectors impose a uniform global receptive field across regions with varying information density, causing local feature degradation especially in dense microscopic target regions. The paper aims to address this mechanistic limitation without architectural modification.
AI.method: ViCrop-Det introduces adaptive spatial trust region shrinkage using Spatial Attention Entropy (SAE) from the decoder's cross-attention as an endogenous probe. It dynamically routes a fixed computational budget to regions with high target saliency and cognitive uncertainty, injecting high-frequency localized observations.
AI.result: +1-3 mAP@50 on VisDrone and DOTA-v1.5 over RT-DETR-R50 and Deformable DETR with 20-23% latency overhead. On COCO, AP_S improves while AP_M/AP_L remain stable. Outperforms uniform slicing under compute-matched settings.
AI.conclusion: Adaptive spatial routing via attention entropy effectively resolves local feature degradation in dense detection, achieving a highly optimized accuracy-speed trade-off without retraining or architectural changes.

title: Bridge: Basis-Driven Causal Inference Marries VFMs for Domain Generalization
summary: Detectors often suffer from degraded performance, primarily due to the distributional gap between the source and target domains. This issue is especially evident in single-source domains with limited data, as models tend to rely on confounders (e.g., illumination, co-occurrence, and style) from the source domain, leading to spurious correlations that hinder generalization. To this end, this paper proposes a novel Basis-driven framework for domain generalization, namely \textbf{\textit{Bridge}}, that incorporates causal inference into object detection. By learning the low-rank bases for front-door adjustment, \textbf{\textit{Bridge}} blocks confounders' effects to mitigate spurious correlations, while simultaneously refining representations by filtering redundant and task-irrelevant components. \textbf{\textit{Bridge}} can be seamlessly integrated with both discriminative (e.g., DINOv2/3, SAM) and generative (e.g., Stable Diffusion) Vision Foundation Models (VFMs). Extensive experiments across multiple domain generalization object detection datasets, i.e., Cross-Camera, Adverse Weather, Real-to-Artistic, Diverse Weather Datasets, and Diverse Weather DroneVehicle (our newly augmented real-world UAV-based benchmark), underscore the superiority of our proposed method over previous state-of-the-art approaches. The project page is available at: https://mingbohong.github.io/Bridge/.

AI.tldr: Bridge is a causal-inference-based framework for domain generalization in object detection that uses low-rank bases for front-door adjustment to block confounders (illumination, co-occurrence, style), reducing spurious correlations. It integrates with both discriminative (DINOv2/3, SAM) and generative (Stable Diffusion) VFMs, and achieves SOTA on multiple benchmarks including a newly created UAV dataset.
AI.motivation: Object detectors suffer from distributional gaps between source and target domains, especially in single-source domains with limited data. Models learn spurious correlations from confounders (illumination, co-occurrence, style) in the source domain, which hinders generalization to unseen domains.
AI.method: Proposes a causal-inference-based framework (Bridge) that learns low-rank bases for front-door adjustment to block confounders' effects, mitigating spurious correlations while filtering redundant and task-irrelevant components. Compatible with both discriminative and generative Vision Foundation Models.
AI.result: Outperforms prior state-of-the-art methods across multiple benchmarks: Cross-Camera, Adverse Weather, Real-to-Artistic, Diverse Weather Datasets, and a newly augmented UAV-based benchmark (Diverse Weather DroneVehicle).
AI.conclusion: Bridge effectively addresses domain generalization in object detection via causal front-door adjustment with low-rank bases, and shows strong compatibility with modern VFMs while achieving superior performance across diverse domain shifts.

title: Edge AI for Automotive Vulnerable Road User Safety: Deployable Detection via Knowledge Distillation
summary: Deploying accurate object detection for Vulnerable Road User (VRU) safety on edge hardware requires balancing model capacity against computational constraints. Large models achieve high accuracy but fail under INT8 quantization required for edge deployment, while small models sacrifice detection performance. This paper presents a knowledge distillation (KD) framework that trains a compact YOLOv8-S student (11.2M parameters) to mimic a YOLOv8-L teacher (43.7M parameters), achieving 3.9x compression while preserving quantization robustness. We evaluate on full-scale BDD100K (70K training images) with Post-Training Quantization to INT8. The teacher suffers catastrophic degradation under INT8 (-23% mAP), while the KD student retains accuracy (-5.6% mAP). Analysis reveals that KD transfers precision calibration rather than raw detection capacity: the KD student achieves 0.748 precision versus 0.653 for direct training at INT8, a 14.5% gain at equivalent recall, reducing false alarms by 44% versus the collapsed teacher. At INT8, the KD student exceeds the teacher's FP32 precision (0.748 vs. 0.718) in a model 3.9x smaller. These findings establish knowledge distillation as a requirement for deploying accurate, safety-critical VRU detection on edge hardware.

AI.tldr: A knowledge distillation framework compresses YOLOv8 from 43.7M to 11.2M parameters (3.9x), enabling accurate INT8-quantized VRU detection on edge hardware. The KD student retains INT8 precision (0.748) exceeding the teacher's FP32 precision (0.718), reducing false alarms by 44% while the collapsed teacher loses 23% mAP under quantization.
AI.motivation: Large object detection models achieve high accuracy but fail under INT8 quantization required for edge deployment, while small models sacrifice detection performance. There is a need to balance model capacity against computational constraints for VRU safety on edge hardware.
AI.method: Knowledge distillation (KD) framework trains a compact YOLOv8-S student (11.2M parameters) to mimic a YOLOv8-L teacher (43.7M parameters), achieving 3.9x compression. Evaluated on BDD100K with Post-Training Quantization to INT8.
AI.result: Teacher suffers -23% mAP under INT8; KD student retains accuracy with only -5.6% mAP loss. KD student achieves 0.748 precision vs 0.653 for direct training at INT8 (14.5% gain), reduces false alarms by 44% versus the collapsed teacher. KD student's INT8 precision (0.748) exceeds teacher's FP32 precision (0.718).
AI.conclusion: Knowledge distillation transfers precision calibration rather than raw detection capacity, and is established as a requirement for deploying accurate, safety-critical VRU detection on edge hardware.

title: Breaking the Rigid Prior: Towards Articulated 3D Anomaly Detection
summary: Existing 3D anomaly detection methods are built on a rigid prior: normal geometry is pose-invariant and can be canonicalized through registration or alignment. This prior does not hold for articulated objects with hinge or sliding joints, where valid pose changes induce structured geometric variations that cannot be collapsed to a single canonical template, causing pose-induced deformations to be misidentified as anomalies while true structural defects are obscured. No existing benchmark addresses this challenge. We introduce ArtiAD, the first large-scale benchmark for articulated 3D anomaly detection, comprising 15,229 point clouds across 39 object categories with dense joint-angle variations and six structural anomaly types. Each sample is annotated with its joint configuration and part-level motion labels, enabling explicit disentanglement of pose-induced geometry from structural defects. ArtiAD also provides a seen/unseen articulation split to evaluate both interpolation and extrapolation to novel joint configurations. We propose Shape-Pose-Aware Signed Distance Field (SPA-SDF), a baseline that replaces the rigid prior with a continuous pose-conditioned implicit field, factorized into an articulation-independent structural prior and a Fourier-encoded joint embedding. At inference, the articulation state is recovered by minimizing reconstruction energy, and anomalies are identified as point-wise deviations from the learned manifold. SPA-SDF achieves 0.884 object-level AUROC on seen configurations and 0.874 on unseen configurations, substantially outperforming all rigid-based baselines. Our code and benchmark will be publicly released to facilitate future research.

AI.tldr: ArtiAD introduces the first large-scale benchmark (15,229 point clouds, 39 categories) for 3D anomaly detection on articulated objects, and proposes SPA-SDF, a pose-conditioned implicit field baseline that significantly outperforms rigid-based methods.
AI.motivation: Existing 3D anomaly detection methods assume pose-invariant normal geometry via registration/alignment, which fails for articulated objects (e.g., hinges, sliding joints) where valid pose changes cause structured geometric variations misidentified as anomalies.
AI.method: SPA-SDF replaces rigid prior with a continuous pose-conditioned implicit field, factorized into an articulation-independent structural prior and a Fourier-encoded joint embedding. At inference, articulation state is recovered by minimizing reconstruction energy, and anomalies are detected as point-wise deviations.
AI.result: SPA-SDF achieves 0.884 object-level AUROC on seen configurations and 0.874 on unseen configurations, substantially outperforming all rigid-based baselines.
AI.conclusion: Articulated 3D anomaly detection requires explicit disentanglement of pose-induced geometry from structural defects; the proposed benchmark and SPA-SDF baseline demonstrate the effectiveness of pose-conditioned modeling over rigid alignment approaches.

title: Uncertainty-Aware Pedestrian Attribute Recognition via Evidential Deep Learning
summary: We propose UAPAR, an Uncertainty-Aware Pedestrian Attribute Recognition framework. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first EDL-based uncertainty-aware framework for pedestrian attribute recognition (PAR). Unlike conventional deterministic methods, which fail to assess prediction reliability on low-quality samples, UAPAR effectively identifies unreliable predictions and thus enhances system robustness in complex real-world scenarios. To achieve this, UAPAR incorporates Evidential Deep Learning (EDL) into a CLIP-based architecture. Specifically, a Region-Aware Evidence Reasoning module employs cross-attention and spatial prior masks to capture fine-grained local features, which are further processed by an evidence head to estimate attribute-wise epistemic uncertainty. To further enhance training robustness, we develop an uncertainty-guided dual-stage curriculum learning strategy to alleviate the adverse effects of severe label noise during training. Extensive experiments on the PA100K, PETA, RAPv1, and RAPv2 datasets demonstrate that UAPAR achieves competitive or superior performance. Furthermore, qualitative results confirm that the proposed framework generates uncertainty estimates that are predictive of challenging or erroneous samples.

AI.tldr: UAPAR is the first Evidential Deep Learning-based framework for Pedestrian Attribute Recognition that uses uncertainty estimation to handle low-quality samples and label noise, achieving competitive results on multiple datasets.
AI.motivation: Existing deterministic PAR methods fail to assess prediction reliability on low-quality samples, limiting robustness in real-world scenarios. The authors aim to introduce uncertainty-awareness to address this gap.
AI.method: UAPAR integrates Evidential Deep Learning (EDL) into a CLIP-based architecture. It includes a Region-Aware Evidence Reasoning module (cross-attention + spatial prior masks) to capture local features and estimate epistemic uncertainty, plus an uncertainty-guided dual-stage curriculum learning strategy to handle label noise.
AI.result: Achieves competitive or superior performance on PA100K, PETA, RAPv1, and RAPv2 datasets. Qualitative results show uncertainty estimates effectively predict challenging or erroneous samples.
AI.conclusion: UAPAR demonstrates that EDL-based uncertainty estimation effectively improves robustness in PAR tasks, offering reliable prediction assessment for real-world deployment.

title: SEAL: Semantic-aware Single-image Sticker Personalization with a Large-scale Sticker-tag Dataset
comment: The last two authors are co-corresponding authors. Please visit our project page at https://cmlab-korea.github.io/SEAL
summary: Synthesizing a target concept from a single reference image is challenging in diffusion-based personalized text-to-image generation, particularly for sticker personalization where prompts often require explicit attribute edits. With only one reference, test-time fine-tuning (TTF) methods tend to overfit, producing \textit{visual entanglement}, where background artifacts are absorbed into the learned concept, and \textit{structural rigidity}, where the model memorizes reference-specific spatial configurations and loses contextual controllability. To address these issues, we introduce \textbf{SE}mantic-aware single-image sticker person\textbf{AL}ization (\textbf{SEAL}), a plug-and-play, architecture-agnostic adaptation module that integrates into existing personalization pipelines without modifying their U-Net-based diffusion backbones. SEAL applies three components during embedding adaptation: (1) a Semantic-guided Spatial Attention Loss, (2) a Split-merge Token Strategy, and (3) Structure-aware Layer Restriction. To support sticker-domain personalization with attribute-level control, we present StickerBench, a large-scale sticker image dataset with structured tags under a six-attribute schema (Appearance, Emotion, Action, Camera Composition, Style, Background). These annotations provide a consistent interface for varying context while keeping target identity fixed, enabling systematic evaluation of identity disentanglement and contextual controllability. Experiments show that SEAL consistently improves identity preservation while maintaining contextual controllability, highlighting the importance of explicit spatial and structural constraints during test-time adaptation. The code, StickerBench, and project page will be publicly released.

AI.tldr: SEAL is a plug-and-play module for diffusion-based personalized text-to-image generation that addresses visual entanglement and structural rigidity in single-image sticker personalization, using semantic-guided spatial attention loss, split-merge token strategy, and structure-aware layer restriction. It also introduces StickerBench, a large sticker dataset with six-attribute annotations for evaluation.
AI.motivation: Synthesizing a target concept from a single reference image in sticker personalization is challenging due to two key problems: (1) visual entanglement (background artifacts absorbed into the learned concept) and (2) structural rigidity (loss of contextual controllability from memorized spatial configurations).
AI.method: SEAL (Semantic-aware single-image sticker personalization) introduces three components during embedding adaptation: (1) Semantic-guided Spatial Attention Loss, (2) Split-merge Token Strategy, and (3) Structure-aware Layer Restriction. It is a plug-and-play, architecture-agnostic module that integrates into existing U-Net-based diffusion backbones without modification.
AI.result: SEAL consistently improves identity preservation while maintaining contextual controllability in sticker personalization tasks. The paper also introduces StickerBench, a large-scale sticker dataset with structured six-attribute tags for systematic evaluation.
AI.conclusion: Explicit spatial and structural constraints during test-time adaptation are crucial for disentangling identity from context. SEAL demonstrates that plug-and-play adaptation can effectively address overfitting issues in single-reference personalization.

title: Graph-based Semantic Calibration Network for Unaligned UAV RGBT Image Semantic Segmentation and A Large-scale Benchmark
summary: Fine-grained RGBT image semantic segmentation is crucial for all-weather unmanned aerial vehicle (UAV) scene understanding. However, UAV RGBT semantic segmentation faces two coupled challenges: cross-modal spatial misalignment caused by sensor parallax and platform vibration, and severe semantic confusion among fine-grained ground objects under top-down aerial views. To address these issues, we propose a Graph-based Semantic Calibration Network (GSCNet) for unaligned UAV RGBT image semantic segmentation. Specifically, we design a Feature Decoupling and Alignment Module (FDAM) that decouples each modality into shared structural and private perceptual components and performs deformable alignment in the shared subspace, enabling robust spatial correction with reduced modality appearance interference. Moreover, we propose a Semantic Graph Calibration Module (SGCM) that explicitly encodes the hierarchical taxonomy and co-occurrence regularities among ground-object categories in UAV scenes into a structured category graph, and incorporates these priors into graph-attention reasoning to calibrate predictions of visually similar and rare categories.In addition, we construct the Unaligned RGB-Thermal Fine-grained (URTF) benchmark, to the best of our knowledge, the largest and most fine-grained benchmark for unaligned UAV RGBT image semantic segmentation, containing over 25,000 image pairs across 61 categories with realistic cross-modal misalignment. Extensive experiments on URTF demonstrate that GSCNet significantly outperforms state-of-the-art methods, with notable gains on fine-grained categories. The dataset is available at https://github.com/mmic-lcl/Datasets-and-benchmark-code.

AI.tldr: GSCNet addresses cross-modal spatial misalignment and semantic confusion in fine-grained UAV RGBT image segmentation via a feature decoupling/alignment module and a semantic graph calibration module, validated on the new URTF benchmark with 25,000+ pairs across 61 categories.
AI.motivation: Fine-grained RGBT semantic segmentation for UAV scenes faces two coupled challenges: cross-modal spatial misalignment (sensor parallax & platform vibration) and severe semantic confusion among fine-grained ground objects under top-down aerial views. Existing methods lack solutions for both simultaneously.
AI.method: Proposes GSCNet with two core modules: (1) Feature Decoupling and Alignment Module (FDAM) that decouples each modality into shared structural and private perceptual components and performs deformable alignment in the shared subspace; (2) Semantic Graph Calibration Module (SGCM) that encodes hierarchical taxonomy and co-occurrence regularities into a structured category graph, using graph-attention reasoning to calibrate predictions. Also constructs the URTF benchmark (25,000+ pairs, 61 categories) with realistic misalignment.
AI.result: GSCNet significantly outperforms state-of-the-art methods on the URTF benchmark, with notable gains on fine-grained categories.
AI.conclusion: The proposed graph-based semantic calibration approach effectively resolves both spatial misalignment and semantic confusion in unaligned UAV RGBT segmentation, and the URTF benchmark provides a valuable resource for future research.

title: AnimateAnyMesh++: A Flexible 4D Foundation Model for High-Fidelity Text-Driven Mesh Animation
summary: Recent advances in 4D content generation have attracted increasing attention, yet creating high-quality animated 3D models remains challenging due to the complexity of modeling spatio-temporal distributions and the scarcity of 4D training data. We present AnimateAnyMesh++, a feed-forward framework for text-driven animation of arbitrary 3D meshes with substantial upgrades in data, architecture, and generative capability. First, we expand the DyMesh-XL dataset by mining dynamic content from Objaverse-XL, increasing the number of unique identities from 60K to 300K and substantially broadening category and motion diversity. Second, we redesign DyMeshVAE-Flex with power-law topology-aware attention and vertex-normal enhanced features, which significantly improves trajectory reconstruction, local geometry preservation, and mitigates trajectory-sticking artifacts. Third, we introduce architectural changes to both DyMeshVAE-Flex and the rectified-flow (RF) generator to support variable-length sequence training and generation, enabling longer animations while preserving reconstruction fidelity. Extensive experiments demonstrate that AnimateAnyMesh++ generates semantically accurate and temporally coherent mesh animations within seconds, surpassing prior approaches in quality and efficiency. The enlarged DyMesh-XL, the upgraded DyMeshVAE-Flex, and variable-length RF together deliver consistent gains across benchmarks and in-the-wild meshes. We will release code, models, and the expanded DyMesh-XL upon acceptance of this manuscript to facilitate research in 4D content creation.

AI.tldr: AnimateAnyMesh++ is a feed-forward framework for text-driven 3D mesh animation, featuring a scaled-up dataset (300K identities), a redesigned VAE with power-law attention, and variable-length sequence support, achieving high-quality and efficient 4D content generation.
AI.motivation: Creating high-quality animated 3D models remains challenging due to the complexity of modeling spatio-temporal distributions and the scarcity of 4D training data.
AI.method: (1) Expanded DyMesh-XL dataset (60K→300K identities); (2) Redesigned DyMeshVAE-Flex with power-law topology-aware attention and vertex-normal enhanced features; (3) Architectural changes for variable-length sequence training and generation using rectified-flow (RF) generator.
AI.result: Generates semantically accurate and temporally coherent mesh animations within seconds, surpassing prior approaches in quality and efficiency, with consistent gains across benchmarks and in-the-wild meshes.
AI.conclusion: The enlarged dataset, upgraded VAE, and variable-length RF together deliver substantial improvements for 4D content creation; code, models, and dataset will be released.

title: Color-Encoded Illumination for High-Speed Volumetric Scene Reconstruction
comment: accepted to IEEE CVPR 2026 as a highlight
summary: The task of capturing and rendering 3D dynamic scenes from 2D images has become increasingly popular in recent years. However, most conventional cameras are bandwidth-limited to 30-60 FPS, restricting these methods to static or slowly evolving scenes. While overcoming bandwidth limitations is difficult for general scenes, recent years have seen a flurry of computational imaging methods that yield high-speed videos using conventional cameras for specific applications (e.g., motion capture and particle image velocimetry). However, most of these methods require modifications to a camera's optics or the addition of mechanically moving components, limiting them to a single-view high-speed capture. Consequently, these methods cannot be readily used to capture a 3D representation of rapid scene motion. In this paper, we propose a novel method to capture and reconstruct a volumetric representation of a high-speed scene using only unaugmented low-speed cameras. Instead of modifying the hardware or optics of each individual camera, we encode high-speed scene dynamics by illuminating the scene with a rapid, sequential color-coded sequence. This results in simultaneous multi-view capture of the scene, where high-speed temporal information is encoded in the spatial intensity and color variations of the captured images. To construct a high-speed volumetric representation of the dynamic scene, we develop a novel dynamic Gaussian Splatting-based approach that decodes the temporal information from the images. We evaluate our approach on simulated scenes and real-world experiments using a multi-camera imaging setup, showing first-of-a-kind high-speed volumetric scene reconstructions.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a method to capture and reconstruct high-speed 3D dynamic scenes using only low-speed conventional cameras by encoding temporal information through rapid color-coded illumination and decoding it with a dynamic Gaussian Splatting approach.
AI.motivation: Conventional cameras are bandwidth-limited to 30-60 FPS, restricting 3D dynamic scene capture to static or slowly evolving scenes. Existing high-speed computational imaging methods require hardware modifications or mechanical components and are limited to single-view capture, preventing 3D volumetric reconstruction of rapid motion.
AI.method: The method encodes high-speed scene dynamics by illuminating the scene with a rapid, sequential color-coded sequence, allowing temporal information to be captured as spatial intensity and color variations in low-speed multi-view images. A dynamic Gaussian Splatting-based approach then decodes this temporal information to reconstruct high-speed volumetric representations.
AI.result: The approach was evaluated on simulated scenes and real-world experiments using a multi-camera imaging setup, demonstrating first-of-a-kind high-speed volumetric scene reconstructions without requiring hardware modifications to the cameras.
AI.conclusion: High-speed 3D dynamic scene reconstruction can be achieved using only unaugmented low-speed cameras by combining color-coded illumination with a dynamic Gaussian Splatting decoder, eliminating the need for specialized high-speed hardware.

title: World2VLM: Distilling World Model Imagination into VLMs for Dynamic Spatial Reasoning
comment: The code is available at https://github.com/WanyueZhang-ai/World2VLM. The dataset is available at https://huggingface.co/datasets/WanyueZhang/World2VLM
summary: Vision-language models (VLMs) have shown strong performance on static visual understanding, yet they still struggle with dynamic spatial reasoning that requires imagining how scenes evolve under egocentric motion. Recent efforts address this limitation either by scaling spatial supervision with synthetic data or by coupling VLMs with world models at inference time. However, the former often lacks explicit modeling of motion-conditioned state transitions, while the latter incurs substantial computational overhead. In this work, we propose World2VLM, a training framework that distills spatial imagination from a generative world model into a vision-language model. Given an initial observation and a parameterized camera trajectory, we use a view-consistent world model to synthesize geometrically aligned future views and derive structured supervision for both forward (action-to-outcome) and inverse (outcome-to-action) spatial reasoning. We post-train the VLM with a two-stage recipe on a compact dataset generated by this pipeline and evaluate it on multiple spatial reasoning benchmarks. World2VLM delivers consistent improvements over the base model across diverse benchmarks, including SAT-Real, SAT-Synthesized, VSI-Bench, and MindCube. It also outperforms the test-time world-model-coupled methods while eliminating the need for expensive inference-time generation. Our results suggest that world models can serve not only as inference-time tools, but also as effective training-time teachers, enabling VLMs to internalize spatial imagination in a scalable and efficient manner.

AI.tldr: World2VLM distills spatial imagination from a generative world model into a VLM via a training framework, enabling dynamic spatial reasoning without costly inference-time generation.
AI.motivation: VLMs struggle with dynamic spatial reasoning (imagining scene evolution under egocentric motion). Existing solutions either lack explicit motion-conditioned state transition modeling or incur substantial computational overhead.
AI.method: Proposes World2VLM: uses a view-consistent world model to synthesize geometrically aligned future views from initial observation + camera trajectory, derives structured supervision for forward and inverse spatial reasoning, then post-trains VLM with a two-stage recipe on generated compact dataset.
AI.result: Consistent improvements over base model on SAT-Real, SAT-Synthesized, VSI-Bench, and MindCube. Outperforms test-time world-model-coupled methods while eliminating expensive inference-time generation.
AI.conclusion: World models can serve effectively as training-time teachers rather than just inference-time tools, enabling VLMs to internalize spatial imagination scalably and efficiently.

title: ProcFunc: Function-Oriented Abstractions for Procedural 3D Generation in Python
summary: We introduce ProcFunc, a library for Blender-based procedural 3D generation in Python. ProcFunc provides a library of easy-to-use Python functions, which streamline creating, combining, analyzing, and executing procedural generation code. ProcFunc makes it easy to create large-scale diverse training data, by combinatorial compositions of semantic components. VLMs can use ProcFunc to edit procedural material and geometry code and can create new procedural code with significantly fewer coding errors. Finally, as an example use case, we use ProcFunc to develop a new procedural generator of indoor rooms, which includes a collection of new compositional procedural materials. We demonstrate the detail, runtime efficiency, and diversity of this room generator, as well as its use for 3D synthetic data generation. Please visit https://github.com/princeton-vl/procfunc for source code.

AI.tldr: ProcFunc is a Python library for procedural 3D generation in Blender that simplifies creating, combining, and analyzing procedural code, enables large-scale diverse training data generation, reduces VLM coding errors, and includes a new procedural indoor room generator.
AI.motivation: To address the need for large-scale, diverse 3D training data and reduce coding errors when VLMs generate procedural content.
AI.method: Development of a Blender-based Python library with easy-to-use functions for procedural 3D generation, supporting combinatorial composition of semantic components and enabling VLM-based code editing/creation.
AI.result: A new procedural indoor room generator with compositional materials; demonstrated detail, runtime efficiency, and diversity for 3D synthetic data generation.
AI.conclusion: ProcFunc effectively streamlines procedural 3D generation, improves VLM code accuracy, and produces high-quality diverse synthetic data.

title: Three-Step Nav: A Hierarchical Global-Local Planner for Zero-Shot Vision-and-Language Navigation
summary: Breakthrough progress in vision-based navigation through unknown environments has been achieved by using multimodal large language models (MLLMs). These models can plan a sequence of motions by evaluating the current view at each time step against the task and goal given to the agent. However, current zero-shot Vision-and-Language Navigation (VLN) agents powered by MLLMs still tend to drift off course, halt prematurely, and achieve low overall success rates. We propose Three-Step Nav to counteract these failures with a three-view protocol: First, "look forward" to extract global landmarks and sketch a coarse plan. Then, "look now" to align the current visual observation with the next sub-goal for fine-grained guidance. Finally, "look backward" audits the entire trajectory to correct accumulated drift before stopping. Requiring no gradient updates or task-specific fine-tuning, our planner drops into existing VLN pipelines with minimal overhead. Three-Step Nav achieves state-of-the-art zero-shot performance on the R2R-CE and RxR-CE dataset. Our code is available at https://github.com/ZoeyZheng0/3-step-Nav.

AI.tldr: Three-Step Nav improves zero-shot VLN by using a three-view protocol (look forward, look now, look backward) to reduce drifting and early stopping, achieving SOTA on R2R-CE and RxR-CE without fine-tuning.
AI.motivation: Current MLLM-based zero-shot VLN agents suffer from drifting off course, premature halting, and low success rates.
AI.method: Three-Step Nav uses three views: (1) "look forward" for global landmark extraction and coarse planning, (2) "look now" for fine-grained sub-goal alignment, and (3) "look backward" for trajectory auditing and drift correction. No gradient updates or fine-tuning required.
AI.result: Achieves state-of-the-art zero-shot performance on R2R-CE and RxR-CE datasets.
AI.conclusion: A simple, drop-in three-view protocol can significantly mitigate common failure modes in MLLM-based VLN without additional training.
